rapidus: Consortium of 32 Japanese firms (Toyota, Sony, SoftBank, Kioxia, NTT, NEC, MUFG, others)
google: Founders — voting power via Class B (economic ~11.5%)
meta: Zuckerberg — voting power via Class B (economic ~13.5%)

policies.name: U.S. BIS AI-chip & SME export controls
CHIPS Act / EU Chips Act incentives

events.title: U.S. expands AI-chip export-control rules
events.summary: New BIS rules widen licensing requirements for top-end AI accelerators and certain HBM stacks shipped to China.
events.first: Chinese firms face tighter access to leading-edge AI chips and HBM.
events.second: Demand shifts to domestic substitutes; pressure on Huawei/SMIC roadmap; retaliation risk via materials.
events.watch: Final BIS rule text · NVIDIA/AMD guidance · Chinese policy response
events.detail: The interim final rule extends license requirements from training-class accelerators to a performance-density band capturing several previously exportable inference parts, and for the first time places certain HBM3E-class memory stacks under the same regime. A 30-day comment window applies; enforcement guidance suggests presumption of denial for named Chinese entities.
events.source: Federal Register · BIS interim final rule (official) + two trade-press confirmations
timeline: Draft rule reported by trade press (conf: Low)
Expected effective date after comment window

events.title: TSMC accelerates CoWoS capacity expansion
events.summary: Reported pull-in of advanced-packaging capacity targets on sustained AI accelerator demand.
events.second: Relieves HBM-attach constraints; pricing pressure on ASE/Amkor; substrate order growth.
events.watch: Monthly CoWoS run-rate estimates · ABF substrate guidance
events.detail: Supply-chain checks point to a pull-forward of 2027 CoWoS-L capacity targets into late 2026, driven by sustained multi-year accelerator commitments. Not confirmed by company IR; magnitude estimates vary ±2 months across sources.
events.source: Taiwan supply-chain press + component-order checks (unconfirmed by TSMC IR)
timeline: Capacity pull-in reported by two independent outlets

events.title: Elevated military activity around Taiwan Strait
events.summary: Increased exercise tempo raises baseline geopolitical risk for the Taiwan cluster.
events.first: No supply disruption; insurance and logistics premiums rise.
events.second: Customer pressure for diversification; tailwind for U.S./Japan fab projects.
events.watch: PLA exercise announcements · TSMC Arizona/Kumamoto ramp commentary
events.detail: Exercise tempo around the median line has risen above its 12-month average for a second consecutive week. No interference with commercial shipping or airfreight observed; the effect is on risk pricing (war-risk insurance, diversification pressure), not physical flows.
events.source: Taiwan MND daily activity reports (official) + shipping-insurance market data
timeline: Second consecutive week elevated; insurers reprice

events.title: China tightens gallium / germanium licensing
events.summary: Slower export-license approvals for Ga/Ge compounds used in power and RF semiconductors.
events.first: Price rises and longer lead times for compound-semiconductor inputs.
events.second: Accelerated non-China sourcing; signals material leverage in tech disputes.
events.watch: License approval rates · Ga/Ge spot prices · MOFCOM statements
events.detail: Approval times for gallium and germanium compound export licenses have lengthened from ~45 to ~75 days per importer reports, without a formal rule change — an administrative-throttling pattern consistent with earlier episodes. Spot prices are up double digits since the slowdown began.
events.source: MOFCOM notices (official) + importer surveys + customs statistics
timeline: Delays confirmed across multiple buyers; prices move (conf: High)

events.title: Major memory maker trims 2026 capex
events.summary: Guidance revision shifts spend from legacy DRAM toward HBM lines.
events.first: Equipment makers exposed to legacy DRAM see order softness.
events.second: HBM concentration deepens; legacy DRAM pricing firms.
events.detail: The revision cuts legacy-DRAM wafer-fab equipment spend while re-allocating to HBM TSV and advanced-packaging lines — net capex roughly flat, mix radically different. Equipment exposure splits: legacy-DRAM deposition/etch softens, bonder and TSV toolmakers benefit.
events.source: Company guidance call transcript + sell-side capex trackers

events.title: Japan approves next Rapidus funding tranche
events.summary: Additional subsidies for 2nm pilot line and packaging R&D.
events.second: Long-run diversification of leading-edge capacity away from Taiwan cluster.
events.watch: Rapidus pilot yields · METI budget details · customer commitments
events.detail: The tranche funds the 2nm pilot line's second phase and a new advanced-packaging R&D consortium, conditional on pilot-yield milestones. Cumulative public support now exceeds ¥1.7T; first customer commitments remain the key open question.
events.source: METI budget announcement (official) + Rapidus press release

scenarios.name: Baseline
scenarios.desc: Current sample state. No simulated shock applied.

scenarios.name: Taiwan Strait crisis
scenarios.desc: Blockade-level disruption injected as a severity-10 simulated event.

scenarios.name: China materials ban
scenarios.desc: Hard export ban on Ga/Ge and rare-earth inputs (severity 9).

scenarios.name: Export controls max
scenarios.desc: Controls extended to sub-leading-edge tools and cloud access (severity 8).

dataNotes.note: ASML ≈83-84% of lithography unit sales (2024-2025); on a revenue basis, materially higher given EUV's ASP premium over Nikon/Canon's DUV-only lineup. ASML holds 100% of EUV specifically.
dataNotes.source: TrendForce 'ASML EUV Dominance' (2025-2026); ASML FY2025 20-F; Nikon/Canon FY2025 segment financials.

dataNotes.note: TSMC ≈70% of total foundry revenue (Q4 2025), ~85-90%+ of leading-edge (<=7nm) capacity specifically.
dataNotes.source: TrendForce press release 20260312-12965 (4Q25 Global Top 10 Foundries).

dataNotes.note: U.S. government converted $8.9B of CHIPS Act/Secure Enclave funding into a 9.9% common-equity stake in Intel, Aug 2025; SoftBank ($2B) and Nvidia ($5B) also took new common-stock positions the same year.
dataNotes.source: Intel 8-K filings (SEC EDGAR, Aug 18 & Sept 15 2025); NPR/CNBC/Manufacturing Dive, Aug 2025.

dataNotes.note: Nvidia became TSMC's #1 customer in 2025 at 19% of revenue, overtaking Apple (17%, down from 22-25% in 2024).
dataNotes.source: CNBC, 'Nvidia set to supplant Apple as TSMC's top customer' (2026-01-26); TSMC FY2025 customer disclosures.

dataNotes.note: SK hynix ~52-55% of HBM (revenue/shipment blend); Samsung materially lower than the prior sample figure (~20-22%, trailed on HBM3E qualification); Micron the fastest riser (~23-25%, targeting 24%+ share by end-2025).
dataNotes.source: Astute Group HBM market share note (2026); TrendForce Micron HBM coverage (2025-06-26); SK hynix 2026 Market Outlook.

dataNotes.note: NVIDIA drove ~27% of SK hynix's total company revenue in 1H2025 (up from ~16% in 2024).
dataNotes.source: TrendForce, 'NVIDIA Reportedly Drives 27% of SK hynix Revenue in 1H25' (2025-08-18).

dataNotes.note: Ansys is no longer an independent EDA competitor — Synopsys completed its acquisition in 2025; Ansys revenue is now consolidated into Synopsys's reported figures.
dataNotes.source: Synopsys FY2025 disclosures; SemiAnalysis EDA Market Primer (2025).

dataNotes.note: NVIDIA's share of the merchant AI-accelerator market is commonly cited around ~75-80% in 2025, well above the prior sample figure.
dataNotes.source: Silicon Analysts, 'NVIDIA AI GPU Market Share 2024-2026'.

dataNotes.note: CXMT (DRAM) and YMTC (NAND) are distinct Chinese memory makers previously merged into one model entry; CXMT is the fastest-growing name in the memory cluster (~7.7% global DRAM share, Q4 2025, rising).
dataNotes.source: SemiAnalysis, 'China's CXMT Is Set to Challenge DRAM Incumbents' (2025-2026).

dataNotes.note: Taiwan's National Development Fund is TSMC's largest single shareholder (~6.4%); GIC (Singapore) is a top-5 holder ahead of the previously modeled 'Capital Group' position.
dataNotes.source: TSMC 2025 Annual Report; MarketScreener shareholder data (2025).

dataNotes.note: Terry Gou's Foxconn stake is reported as mid-single-digit and declining; holding companies linked to his daughter disclosed disposing of all their Hon Hai shares in Dec 2025.
Foxconn's cloud/AI-server segment overtook Apple-driven consumer electronics as its largest revenue segment for the first time in 2025.
dataNotes.source: CRN Asia (Jan 2026); Hon Hai FY2025/Q1 2026 results.

dataNotes.note: US hyperscale AI-data-center concentration (~60%+) is understated by a flat 0.5/0.25 US/China split; China's real 2025-2026 AI capex is far smaller in absolute terms than any single US hyperscaler's guided 2026 capex. Per-hyperscaler ordering corrected to match guided 2026 capex (Amazon $200B > Google $175-185B > Meta $115-135B > Microsoft $110-120B) — the prior sample had Google above Amazon and Microsoft above Meta, the reverse of guided spending; total US-hyperscaler share held at the same ~57% (of the combined stage total) used before, just reallocated among the four in the correct order.
dataNotes.source: Company earnings-call capex guidance (Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, Microsoft, Q4 2025/Q1 2026 calls); aggregated in 'Hyperscalers Plan $630 Billion in 2026 CapEx' and 'Big Tech AI Capex 2026' trackers (early 2026).

dataNotes.note: Final-systems-assembly (ODM/EMS) shares corrected to match 2025Q4/2026 AI-server assembly share: Foxconn ~40% (was modeled at 32%), Quanta ~20-30% as the clear #2 (was modeled at only 9% — below Wistron, the reverse of reality), Wistron ~8-10% (was modeled at 12%, overstated relative to Quanta). Supermicro and Luxshare shares nudged down slightly for consistency; Supermicro in particular is a branded server vendor/design house, not a pure contract ODM, so its share here is a lower-confidence estimate.
dataNotes.source: DigiTimes, 'Foxconn, Wistron, Quanta to sustain trillion-dollar revenue on AI server in 2026' (2026-01-09); Omdia AI-server-rack-assembly market-share estimates (2025-2026).

dataNotes.note: SPIL has been a wholly-owned ASE Technology Holding subsidiary since 2018 — its output is already consolidated into ASE's reported financials; modeled here without an independent stake to avoid double-counting.
dataNotes.source: ASE Technology Holding corporate structure disclosures.

dataNotes.note: "memory_fab" models DRAM and NAND as one combined stage, but each company's earlier entry was its own single-segment (DRAM-only or NAND-only) share entered directly against that combined denominator — e.g. Kioxia (NAND-only) was carrying its ~14-16% NAND share as if it were a share of the combined market, and the modeled total summed to >100%. Corrected to a revenue-weighted blend of Q1 2026 DRAM shares (Samsung 38%, SK hynix 29%, Micron 22%, CXMT 8%, Nanya 2%) and NAND shares (Samsung 29%, SK hynix 18%, Kioxia 14%, Micron 13%, YMTC 13%), weighted by the real DRAM:NAND market-size split (~$129B:$65B, ~66.5%:33.5%). The ~4.5% left over is real (chiefly Western Digital/SanDisk's NAND share) but not modeled as its own company here, so it is carried as unmodeled residual rather than invented.
dataNotes.source: Counterpoint Research, "Global DRAM and HBM Market Share" and "Global NAND Memory Market Share" (Q1 2026); Yole Group, "Memory market surges beyond expectations: almost $200 billion in 2025 driven by HBM & AI" (2025).
